files.SKILL.md:
---
name: execution-protocol
description: Disk-first execution protocol. Discover before building (read spec/verifier/sources first). Stub immediately. Preserve volatile state by copying before first access. Implement at 60% confidence, verify often, fix literally. Change shape after three structural failures. Reproduce all spec literals exactly including dates/paths/formats/whitespace. Read component source before predicting behavior, inspect intermediate pipeline output directly. Restore inaccessible files via native tool not hand-parsing. Validate service config syntax AND live connectivity. Measure reference match quantitatively. Guarantee cleanup runs for every started task. Investigate unknown programs cheap-to-expensive. Fix smallest failing unit. Read disk artifact before stopping.
---

# Execution Protocol

Only what is written to disk matters. In-session
reasoning that produces no artifact changes nothing.

## Execution order

**Discover before building.**
Before writing any solution, read the task specification,
the verifier or test script, and every source file
referenced. The verifier tells you exactly what pass
looks like. Source files tell you exactly how components
behave. Prior knowledge is a starting hypothesis, not
a substitute for reading.

**Stub immediately.**
Create the output artifact with placeholder content
before any other work. All subsequent steps overwrite
this file in place.

**Preserve volatile state.**
Before issuing any command against a stateful resource,
copy it. Resources that maintain transaction logs,
history, or journals often modify themselves on first
access — even read-only access. Copy all associated
files together, then work on the copy.

**Implement early, verify often.**
Save a working attempt at 60% confidence. Run the
verifier's check. Read failures literally. Fix the
specific thing the failure names. Repeat.

**Change shape on repeated failure.**
If the same structural approach fails three times,
the structure is wrong. Diagnose the root cause and
choose a different approach — don't vary parameters
within a failing structure.

## Environment

Shell, Python interpreter, file I/O. No speculative
imports. Use only installed packages and the standard
library.

## Specification compliance

Every literal value in the task description is a
constraint: file paths, function names, date values,
output field names, format characters. Reproduce them
exactly. Automated tests compare against exact values —
there is no partial credit for close approximations
of literals.

Pinned dates must appear as literal values in code,
not as calls to the runtime clock.

Output format must match any provided sample exactly,
including whitespace, separator characters, quoting
style, and trailing newlines.

## Reading components before using them

When a task involves a component you did not write —
a filter, a parser, a sanitizer, a service — read its
source before predicting its behavior. The component's
actual logic frequently differs from what its name or
category implies.

This is especially important when two components
process the same data in sequence. The output of the
first component is the input to the second. Inspect
that intermediate output directly rather than reasoning
about it from first principles.

## Recovering inaccessible data

When a file cannot be opened by its native tool,
investigate why before attempting manual parsing.
The goal is to restore the file to a state the native
tool can read, then use that tool normally.

Gather information from the environment: nearby files,
configuration, environment variables, embedded strings
in binaries, and comments in source files may all
contain the information needed to restore access.
Search broadly before concluding that recovery
information does not exist.

## Validating running services

A script that configures and starts a service must
confirm the service is actually accepting connections
before it exits. Configuration validity and runtime
availability are separate conditions — both must hold.

Directive placement, format string syntax, and literal
values in configuration files are all tested by
automated checks. Match the specification exactly.

## Matching a reference output

When the task requires producing output that matches
a reference, establish a quantitative measure of
similarity early. Sample the reference, derive
generation parameters from measurement rather than
intuition, and verify the similarity metric before
finalizing.

Deterministic generation is preferable to stochastic
generation when operating under time or size
constraints.

## Concurrent execution with cleanup guarantees

When implementing concurrent work with cancellation
semantics, the critical property is that cleanup code
runs for every task that has started, regardless of
how cancellation is triggered. Design and test this
property explicitly — don't assume it holds.

## Investigating unknown programs

Inspect before executing. Read file type, byte
content, and embedded strings first. Request usage
information. Run with minimal input. Observe all
output channels. Escalate to system call tracing
only when lighter methods are insufficient.

## Quality discipline

Fix the smallest unit that resolves the failure.
Validate inputs at trust boundaries. Use stable
time sources for elapsed-time measurement. Write
comments only where the reason is not obvious from
the code.

## Final check

Before stopping, read the artifact on disk. If it
does not reflect your current understanding of the
correct solution, update it. The disk state at
termination is the answer.
